content.full_text:
t

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 31, 1999


Laura Winslow
Office of Registrar of Voters
County of Solano
510 Clay Street
Fairfield, California 94533


Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-294

Dear Ms. Winslow:

     This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ as they pertain to the filing of candidate statements of
economic interests by judges.

QUESTION

     Must incumbent judges who are running for re-election to Solano County Superior Court
file candidate statements of economic interests ("candidate SEIs")?

CONCLUSION

     Yes, incumbent judges must file a candidate SEI with the declaration of candidacy.


_________________________

¹ California Government Code sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections
18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-99-294
Page 2

FACTS

A number of sitting judges in the Solano County Superior Court are up for re-election on
March 7, 2000. Incumbent judges were required to file a declaration of candidacy to run for re-
election no later than December 10, 1999. In addition, Elections Code section 8203 allows a
write-in candidate to qualify for the ballot up to 10 days after the deadline for the declaration of
candidacy expires. If no candidate qualifies to run against an incumbent, he or she continues in
office without appearing on the ballot. A question has been raised whether incumbent judges
who will likely be unopposed for the March ballot must file a candidate SEI with his or her
declaration of candidacy.

ANALYSIS

Elected officials specified in section 87200, including superior court judges, are subject to
the Act's requirements on filing periodic statements of economic interests. They must file
statements of economic interest when they file a declaration of candidacy to run for office, upon
assuming office, annually thereafter while holding office, and upon leaving office. The
requirement for filing a statement of economic interest with a declaration of candidacy is as
follows:

"Every candidate for an office specified in section 87200 other than a justice of an
appellate court or the Supreme Court shall file no later than the final filing date of
a declaration of candidacy, a statement disclosing his or her investments, his or
her interests in real property, and any income received during the immediately
preceding 12 months.

This statement shall not be required if the candidate has filed, within 60 days prior
to the filing of his or her declaration of candidacy, a statement for the same
jurisdiction pursuant to section 87202 or 87203 [assuming office and annual
statement of economic interests, respectively]." (Section 87201.)

Section 82007 defines a "candidate" as:

" . . . an individual who is listed on the ballot or who has qualified to have write-in
votes on his or her behalf counted by election officials, for nomination for or
election to any elective office, or who receives a contribution or makes an
expenditure or gives his or her consent for any other person to receive a

File No. A-99-294
Page 3

contribution or make an expenditure with a view to bringing about his or her
nomination or election to any elective office, whether or not the specific elective
office for which he or she will seek nomination or election is known at the time
the contribution is received or the expenditure is made and whether or not he or
she has announced his or her candidacy or filed a declaration of candidacy at such
time. 'Candidate' also includes any officeholder who is the subject of a recall
election. An individual who becomes a candidate shall retain his or her status as a
candidate until such time as that status is terminated pursuant to Section 84214.
'Candidate' does not include any person within the meaning of Section 301(b) of
the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971."

When the state primary election was in June, many incumbent officeholders (including
judges) who filed their annual statements of economic interests on March 1, did not have to file a
separate candidate SEI. Under section 87201, an incumbent officeholder does not have to file a
separate candidate SEI if he or she has filed an annual statement of economic interest within 60
days prior to filing a declaration of candidacy. But because the state primary election has been
moved up from June to March, many incumbent officeholders, including judges, who would
ordinarily not have to file a separate candidate SEI, must file one.

Section 87201, which requires candidates to file statements of economic interests, does
not provide an exception for incumbents who are running for re-election but who, because they
are unopposed, will not be on the ballot in the election. Adding to the problem is the fact that,
pursuant to Elections Code section 8203, incumbent judges running for re-election do not know
if they are unopposed and therefore off the ballot until after the deadline for filing a candidate
SEI has passed. In addition, the Commission has interpreted the Act's definition of "candidate"
broadly. In the Lui opinion, the Commission stated "all elected officeholders are 'candidates,'
even during a non-election year." (In re Lui, 10 FPPC Ops. 10; No. 87-001.) Therefore,
incumbent judges must file a candidate SEI no later than the final deadline for filing the
declaration of candidacy when they are filing for re-election to their current office.

The early primary means many incumbent officeholders who previously filed only an
annual SEI must now file an additional candidate SEI. We recognize that this is an additional
burden for some sitting judges who may not even end up being listed on the ballot if no one files
to run against them. These candidates should be aware that they may file an update of the
candidate SEI for their annual statement due March 1, instead of re-filing the entire statement.
They may file a one-page Form 700 certification (copy enclosed) instead of the Form 700 if their
candidate statements contain all the required information for their 1999 annual statements.

File No. A-99-294
Page 4

If you or the judges you work with, have suggestions for a simpler filing requirement that
is consistent with the current statutory scheme for judicial candidates' SEIs, we would appreciate
hearing them. If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant

Enclosure
